revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

[[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

[[Category:ファイル]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}

{{Book-stub}}

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

[[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:Shintō Texts]] [[Category:Occult Books]] [[Category:Japanese Pseudepigrapha]] [[Category:Divination]] [[Category:Abe no Seimei]] [[Category:bump]]
[[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * **Volume 1**: Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * **Volume 2**: Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * **Volume 3**: Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * **Volume 4**: Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * **Volume 5**: Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.

{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.

[[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

[[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Shinnaiden]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Shinnaiden-qq]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."

It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

[[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Shinnaiden]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Shinnaiden-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

[[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Shinnaiden]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] <!--Shinnaiden-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shinnaiden]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}
