'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."

After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>

== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.

{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|方位神}}" to "|方位神|1=Directional deity}}"
revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|牛頭天王}}" to "|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}}"
revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017年8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.

These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.

The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.

{{draft categories| [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Gion Shrines]] [[Category:Shintō Texts]] [[Category:Occult Books]] [[Category:Japanese Pseudepigrapha]] [[Category:Divination]] [[Category:Abe no Seimei]] [[Category:Shinnaiden]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinnaiden]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]] [[en:Shinnaiden]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinnaiden}}

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明|12=draft}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|12=draft}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎|12=draft}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|12=draft}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備|12=draft}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|12=draft}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=draft}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|12=draft}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|12=draft}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|12=draft}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古|12=draft}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|12=draft}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|12=draft}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|12=draft}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|12=draft}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩|12=draft}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

revision.comment: 外部リンクの修正 http:// -> https:// (codh.rois.ac.jp) ([[Wikipedia:Bot|Bot]]による編集)
revision.text:
『'''三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集'''（さんごくそうでんいんようかんかつほきないでんきんうぎょくとしゅう）』は、安部清明（[[安倍晴明]]）が編纂したと伝承される[[占い]]の専門・実用書。実際は晴明死後（成立年代は諸説ある）に作られたものである。
『'''三国相伝宣明暦経註'''（さんごくそうでんせんみょうれきけいちゅう）』ともいい、『'''簠簋内伝'''（ほきないでん）』または『'''簠簋'''（ほき）』、または『'''金烏玉兎集'''（きんうぎょくとしゅう）』と略称される。
ちなみに「簠（{竹甫皿}）簋（{竹艮皿}）」とは、古代中国で用いられた祭器の名称である。
==概要== [[金烏]]は太陽に棲むとも太陽の化身とも言われる三本足の金の烏であり、太陽を象徴する霊鳥である。[[玉兎]]は月に棲むとも言われるウサギで、月を象徴する。すなわちこれは気の循環を知り、日月の運行によって占うという陰陽師の秘伝書であることを象徴している。
==著者について== 著者については安部晴明が仮託されているが、晴明の死後に編集されたものであるため信憑性は低い。
江戸中期の「泰山集」に当時の安倍家陰陽道宗家の当主であった[[土御門泰福|安倍泰福]]の言葉として「簠簋内伝は[[真言宗|真言]]僧が作ったものであり、安倍家伝来のものではない。晴明が伝授したのは[[吉備真備]]が入唐して持ち帰った天文だ」という言が記載されているという。
「日本陰陽道史総説」によれば[[村山修一]]は[[西田長男]]の説明を受け、著者を晴明の子孫にあたる祇園社の祠官とみなしている。晴明の子である[[安倍吉平]]の後、安倍家は時親、円弥、[[安倍泰親|泰親]]の3流に分かれた。その中の円弥の子孫が祇園社に入ったらしく、さらにその子孫の晴朝が簠簋内伝の著者ではないかとしている。
現存する簠簋内伝や刊本をすべて調査した[[中村璋八]]は自著にて「(晴朝が著者であるということは)確実な資料によるものではなく、必ずしも納得のできるものではない」として、簠簋内伝の著者については保留としている。
いずれにせよ、晴明によってなんらかの伝えはあったであろうが著者についてはやはり不明とするのが妥当となる。
==構成== 全5巻で構成されている。 *第1巻は[[牛頭天王]]の縁起と、様々な[[方位神]]とその吉凶を説明している。 *第2巻は世界最初の神・[[盤古]]の縁起と、盤牛王の子らの解説、暦の吉凶を説明している。 *第3巻は1、2巻には書かれなかった[[納音]]、[[空亡]]などが説明されている。 *第4巻は[[風水]]、建築に関する吉凶説をのべている。 *第5巻は[[密教占星術]]である[[宿曜]]占術をのべている。
{{独自研究範囲|1〜3巻と比べて、4〜5巻はあきらかに異質である。最初に1〜2巻が書かれ、それの増補として3巻が加えられた。それに、別個に成立したと思われる本が加えられ、これが4巻、5巻であったと考えられる。|date=2017年8月}}
==天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝== 本書中<ref>国立国会図書館所蔵の田中太右衛門版では巻3の[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 冒頭]。</ref>には、その由来を示す逸話である「天文司郎 安部博士 吉備后胤 清明朝臣入唐伝」が載せられている<ref>『金烏玉兎集』には多くの写本が現存するが、田中太右衛門版のように「天文司郎 安部博士 吉備后胤 清明朝臣入唐伝」の題名のみ記して、内容をすべて省略しているものが存在する。</ref>。
「吉備后胤」とは[[吉備真備]]の後胤にあたることを意味する。
本書の記述では、本書は天竺で[[文殊菩薩]]から伯道上人に伝えられ、これが清明に伝えられたことが記されている。
[[安元]]年中（1175年-1177年）、清明は唐に渡って伯道上人に師事し、本書を授与され日本に帰り書を石の箱の中に納めた。
ある時、清明の妻の利花は清明の[[弟子]]である[[道満]]と不倫関係となり、道満にその書を書き写させた。それから道満は「私は金烏玉兎集を授かった」と清明に告げた。清明は「金烏玉兎集は伯道上人が修行してようやく文殊菩薩から頂いたもの。お前が持っているはずが無い」と言った。道満と清明は言い争い、清明が誤っていたので首を切られた。
同じころ、伯道上人は清明が殺されたことを察知し、日本にやってきた。そして無残に殺された清明の骨を拾い集め、術を掛けて蘇生させた。そして報復をするため、生き返った清明と共に道満の元へ向かった。
伯道は道満に「清明はいるか」と尋ねた。道満は「かつてここにいたが、首を刎ねられて死んだ」と答えた。すると伯道が「先ほど彼と会った」と言った。道満は「そんなはずはない。もし晴明が生きていたらこの首を差し上げよう」と答えた。そのとき晴明がやって来た。こうして道満は約束通り首を刎ねられたという。
このことから女には心を許すなと戒めた。
==簠簋抄== 『簠簋抄』は江戸時代初期までに出版されたと推察されている『金烏玉兎集』の注釈書。『簠簋袖裡伝』という『簠簋抄』よりも一段階古い『金烏玉兎集』の注釈書も存在する。『簠簋抄』の序文「三国相伝簠簋金烏玉兎集之由来」は、『金烏玉兎集』の序文「天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝」の内容を大幅に増補・改変したもので、『金烏玉兎集』の由来を、巷に流布している各種説話、伝承を取り入れて読み物風に仕立てている。
『簠簋抄』の[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2544460/4 記述]では、『金烏玉兎集』は天竺で文殊菩薩が書いたもので、その後伯道上人に伝えられ、これが[[吉備真備]]の手に渡り、その際吉備真備に助力した安部仲丸（遣唐使として唐に渡った後死して鬼と化している）の子孫である清明（『簠簋抄』では「晴明」ではなく「'''清'''明」）に伝えられたことが記されている。
「清明」の名についてはその命名が[[清明節]]に因むと説明されている。
さらに晴明の母はキツネであるとする[[葛の葉]]伝説も『簠簋抄』に記載されたものである。
[[天皇|帝]]の御前で長持の中身を占うという術比べを清明として負けた[[薩摩国|薩摩]]（『簠簋抄』では「[[播磨国|播磨]]」ではなく「薩摩」）の[[道満]]はその後、清明の弟子になったという。
[[近衛天皇]]の代（1142年-1155年）に清明は唐に渡り伯道上人のもとで修行をして帰国していたが、留守中に、道満は清明の妻・利花と不倫関係となっていた。
清明が不在のときに道満は彼女から石の箱のことを聞き出した。しかし、その開け方は妻にも分からないと言う。道満はその箱を見せてもらい、何とかして箱を開け、中にあった金烏玉兎集を書き写してしまった。そして清明が帰宅した時に「私は金烏玉兎集を授かった」と彼に告げた。清明は「お前が持っているはずが無い」と言った。そして嘘を言っている方が首を切られることになった。道満は懐からあらかじめ書き取っておいた金烏玉兎集を見せ、清明の首を刎ね、利花を妻とした。
同じころ、伯道上人は清明が殺されたことを察知し、日本にやってきた。そして殺された清明の骨を拾い集め、術を掛けて蘇生させた。そして報復をするため、生き返った清明と共に道満の元へ向かった。
伯道は道満に清明のことを尋ねると、道満は「首を刎ねられて死んだ」と答えた。すると伯道が「そんなはずは無い。先ほど彼と会った」と言った。道満は「もし清明が生きていたらこの首を差し上げよう」と答えた。そして伯道は先ほど蘇生した清明を呼び道満に見せた。こうして道満は首を刎ねられ、書き写された金烏玉兎集は焼却、利花も殺害されたという。
このことからこの書をみだりに、浅はかな気持ちで読むことは死に値するとして、この書が秘伝中の秘伝であるということをあらわし、この話を冒頭に置くことによってこの書の秘密性・神聖性を高めた。
==刊行本== *[[藤巻一保]]著『安倍晴明『簠簋内伝』現代語訳総解説』[[戎光祥出版]]、2017年、 ISBN 978-4864032636 *[[下出積与]]校註 『[[神道大系]]』 103 陰陽道 * 日本古典偽書叢刊 第３巻（現代思潮新社） ISBN 9784329004321

== 関連項目 == *[[祇園信仰]] *[[八坂神社]]

65 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集 up to rev 104510194
revision.text:
『'''三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集'''（さんごくそうでんいんようかんかつほきないでんきんうぎょくとしゅう）』は、安部清明（[[安倍晴明]]）が編纂したと伝承される[[占い]]の専門・実用書。実際は晴明死後（成立年代は諸説ある）に作られたものである。
『'''三国相伝宣明暦経註'''（さんごくそうでんせんみょうれきけいちゅう）』ともいい、『'''簠簋内伝'''（ほきないでん）』または『'''簠簋'''（ほき）』、または『'''金烏玉兎集'''（きんうぎょくとしゅう）』と略称される。
ちなみに「簠（{竹甫皿}）簋（{竹艮皿}）」とは、古代中国で用いられた祭器の名称である。
==概要== [[金烏]]は太陽に棲むとも太陽の化身とも言われる三本足の金の烏であり、太陽を象徴する霊鳥である。[[玉兎]]は月に棲むとも言われるウサギで、月を象徴する。すなわちこれは気の循環を知り、日月の運行によって占うという陰陽師の秘伝書であることを象徴している。
==著者について== 著者については安部晴明が仮託されているが、晴明の死後に編集されたものであるため信憑性は低い。
江戸中期の「泰山集」に当時の安倍家陰陽道宗家の当主であった[[土御門泰福|安倍泰福]]の言葉として「簠簋内伝は[[真言宗|真言]]僧が作ったものであり、安倍家伝来のものではない。晴明が伝授したのは[[吉備真備]]が入唐して持ち帰った天文だ」という言が記載されているという。
「日本陰陽道史総説」によれば[[村山修一]]は[[西田長男]]の説明を受け、著者を晴明の子孫にあたる祇園社の祠官とみなしている。晴明の子である[[安倍吉平]]の後、安倍家は時親、円弥、[[安倍泰親|泰親]]の3流に分かれた。その中の円弥の子孫が祇園社に入ったらしく、さらにその子孫の晴朝が簠簋内伝の著者ではないかとしている。
現存する簠簋内伝や刊本をすべて調査した[[中村璋八]]は自著にて「(晴朝が著者であるということは)確実な資料によるものではなく、必ずしも納得のできるものではない」として、簠簋内伝の著者については保留としている。
いずれにせよ、晴明によってなんらかの伝えはあったであろうが著者についてはやはり不明とするのが妥当となる。
==構成== 全5巻で構成されている。 *第1巻は[[牛頭天王]]の縁起と、様々な[[方位神]]とその吉凶を説明している。 *第2巻は世界最初の神・[[盤古]]の縁起と、盤牛王の子らの解説、暦の吉凶を説明している。 *第3巻は1、2巻には書かれなかった[[納音]]、[[空亡]]などが説明されている。 *第4巻は[[風水]]、建築に関する吉凶説をのべている。 *第5巻は[[密教占星術]]である[[宿曜]]占術をのべている。
{{独自研究範囲|1〜3巻と比べて、4〜5巻はあきらかに異質である。最初に1〜2巻が書かれ、それの増補として3巻が加えられた。それに、別個に成立したと思われる本が加えられ、これが4巻、5巻であったと考えられる。|date=2017年8月}}
==天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝== 本書中<ref>国立国会図書館所蔵の田中太右衛門版では巻3の[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 冒頭]。</ref>には、その由来を示す逸話である「天文司郎 安部博士 吉備后胤 清明朝臣入唐伝」が載せられている<ref>『金烏玉兎集』には多くの写本が現存するが、田中太右衛門版のように「天文司郎 安部博士 吉備后胤 清明朝臣入唐伝」の題名のみ記して、内容をすべて省略しているものが存在する。</ref>。
「吉備后胤」とは[[吉備真備]]の後胤にあたることを意味する。
本書の記述では、本書は天竺で[[文殊菩薩]]から伯道上人に伝えられ、これが清明に伝えられたことが記されている。
[[安元]]年中（1175年-1177年）、清明は唐に渡って伯道上人に師事し、本書を授与され日本に帰り書を石の箱の中に納めた。
ある時、清明の妻の利花は清明の[[弟子]]である[[道満]]と不倫関係となり、道満にその書を書き写させた。それから道満は「私は金烏玉兎集を授かった」と清明に告げた。清明は「金烏玉兎集は伯道上人が修行してようやく文殊菩薩から頂いたもの。お前が持っているはずが無い」と言った。道満と清明は言い争い、清明が誤っていたので首を切られた。
同じころ、伯道上人は清明が殺されたことを察知し、日本にやってきた。そして無残に殺された清明の骨を拾い集め、術を掛けて蘇生させた。そして報復をするため、生き返った清明と共に道満の元へ向かった。
伯道は道満に「清明はいるか」と尋ねた。道満は「かつてここにいたが、首を刎ねられて死んだ」と答えた。すると伯道が「先ほど彼と会った」と言った。道満は「そんなはずはない。もし晴明が生きていたらこの首を差し上げよう」と答えた。そのとき晴明がやって来た。こうして道満は約束通り首を刎ねられたという。
このことから女には心を許すなと戒めた。
==簠簋抄== 『簠簋抄』は江戸時代初期までに出版されたと推察されている『金烏玉兎集』の注釈書。『簠簋袖裡伝』という『簠簋抄』よりも一段階古い『金烏玉兎集』の注釈書も存在する。『簠簋抄』の序文「三国相伝簠簋金烏玉兎集之由来」は、『金烏玉兎集』の序文「天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝」の内容を大幅に増補・改変したもので、『金烏玉兎集』の由来を、巷に流布している各種説話、伝承を取り入れて読み物風に仕立てている。
『簠簋抄』の[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2544460/4 記述]では、『金烏玉兎集』は天竺で文殊菩薩が書いたもので、その後伯道上人に伝えられ、これが[[吉備真備]]の手に渡り、その際吉備真備に助力した安部仲丸（遣唐使として唐に渡った後死して鬼と化している）の子孫である清明（『簠簋抄』では「晴明」ではなく「'''清'''明」）に伝えられたことが記されている。
「清明」の名についてはその命名が[[清明節]]に因むと説明されている。
さらに晴明の母はキツネであるとする[[葛の葉]]伝説も『簠簋抄』に記載されたものである。
[[天皇|帝]]の御前で長持の中身を占うという術比べを清明として負けた[[薩摩国|薩摩]]（『簠簋抄』では「[[播磨国|播磨]]」ではなく「薩摩」）の[[道満]]はその後、清明の弟子になったという。
[[近衛天皇]]の代（1142年-1155年）に清明は唐に渡り伯道上人のもとで修行をして帰国していたが、留守中に、道満は清明の妻・利花と不倫関係となっていた。
清明が不在のときに道満は彼女から石の箱のことを聞き出した。しかし、その開け方は妻にも分からないと言う。道満はその箱を見せてもらい、何とかして箱を開け、中にあった金烏玉兎集を書き写してしまった。そして清明が帰宅した時に「私は金烏玉兎集を授かった」と彼に告げた。清明は「お前が持っているはずが無い」と言った。そして嘘を言っている方が首を切られることになった。道満は懐からあらかじめ書き取っておいた金烏玉兎集を見せ、清明の首を刎ね、利花を妻とした。
同じころ、伯道上人は清明が殺されたことを察知し、日本にやってきた。そして殺された清明の骨を拾い集め、術を掛けて蘇生させた。そして報復をするため、生き返った清明と共に道満の元へ向かった。
伯道は道満に清明のことを尋ねると、道満は「首を刎ねられて死んだ」と答えた。すると伯道が「そんなはずは無い。先ほど彼と会った」と言った。道満は「もし清明が生きていたらこの首を差し上げよう」と答えた。そして伯道は先ほど蘇生した清明を呼び道満に見せた。こうして道満は首を刎ねられ、書き写された金烏玉兎集は焼却、利花も殺害されたという。
このことからこの書をみだりに、浅はかな気持ちで読むことは死に値するとして、この書が秘伝中の秘伝であるということをあらわし、この話を冒頭に置くことによってこの書の秘密性・神聖性を高めた。
==刊行本== *[[藤巻一保]]著『安倍晴明『簠簋内伝』現代語訳総解説』[[戎光祥出版]]、2017年、 ISBN 978-4864032636 *[[下出積与]]校註 『[[神道大系]]』 103 陰陽道 * 日本古典偽書叢刊 第３巻（現代思潮新社） ISBN 9784329004321

[[天皇|帝]]の御前で長持の中身を占うという術比べを清明として負けた[[薩摩国|薩摩]]（『簠簋抄』では「[[播磨国|播磨]]」ではなく「薩摩」）の[[道満]]はその後、清明の弟子になったという。
[[近衛天皇]]の代（1142年-1155年）に清明は唐に渡り伯道上人のもとで修行をして帰国していたが、留守中に、道満は清明の妻・利花と不倫関係となっていた。
清明が不在のときに道満は彼女から石の箱のことを聞き出した。しかし、その開け方は妻にも分からないと言う。道満はその箱を見せてもらい、何とかして箱を開け、中にあった金烏玉兎集を書き写してしまった。そして清明が帰宅した時に「私は金烏玉兎集を授かった」と彼に告げた。清明は「お前が持っているはずが無い」と言った。そして嘘を言っている方が首を切られることになった。道満は懐からあらかじめ書き取っておいた金烏玉兎集を見せ、清明の首を刎ね、利花を妻とした。
同じころ、伯道上人は清明が殺されたことを察知し、日本にやってきた。そして殺された清明の骨を拾い集め、術を掛けて蘇生させた。そして報復をするため、生き返った清明と共に道満の元へ向かった。
伯道は道満に清明のことを尋ねると、道満は「首を刎ねられて死んだ」と答えた。すると伯道が「そんなはずは無い。先ほど彼と会った」と言った。道満は「もし清明が生きていたらこの首を差し上げよう」と答えた。そして伯道は先ほど蘇生した清明を呼び道満に見せた。こうして道満は首を刎ねられ、書き写された金烏玉兎集は焼却、利花も殺害されたという。
このことからこの書をみだりに、浅はかな気持ちで読むことは死に値するとして、この書が秘伝中の秘伝であるということをあらわし、この話を冒頭に置くことによってこの書の秘密性・神聖性を高めた。
==刊行本== *[[藤巻一保]]著『安倍晴明『簠簋内伝』現代語訳総解説』[[戎光祥出版]]、2017年、 ISBN 978-4864032636 *[[下出積与]]校註 『[[神道大系]]』 103 陰陽道 * 日本古典偽書叢刊 第３巻（現代思潮新社） ISBN 9784329004321
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[祇園信仰]] *[[八坂神社]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽&viewRestricted=0 国会図書館所蔵 三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集 一覧] *[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=%E7%B0%A0%E7%B0%8B%E6%8A%84&viewRestricted=0 国会図書館所蔵 簠簋抄 一覧] *[https://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ 簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集] - 人文学オープンデータ共同利用センター *[https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 『簠簋内伝』の宗教世界] *[https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 『簠簋内伝』ノート：牛頭天王縁起説話との関連から] {{Book-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:さんこくそうてんいんようかんかつほきないてんきんうきよくとしゆう}} [[Category:陰陽道]] [[Category:祇園神社|+さんこくそうてんいんようかんかつほきないてんきんうきよくとしゆう]] [[Category:神道の文献]] [[Category:オカルトの書籍]] [[Category:日本の偽書]] [[Category:占い]] [[Category:安倍晴明]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎|1=Yutu|2=ja|3=玉兎|lt=Gyokuto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備|qq=draft|qq=draft}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]
{{Book-stub}}

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Seimei|qq=}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎|1=Yutu|2=ja|3=玉兎|lt=Gyokuto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutu|qq=}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Makibi|qq=}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|qq=}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pangu|qq=}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mañjuśrī|qq=}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Faith|ja|祇園信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Faith|qq=}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

[[sn:Shinnaiden]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]] [[en:Shinnaiden]]
<!--Shinnaiden--> {{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Gion Faith]] * [[Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū editions] * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=%E7%B0%A0%E7%B0%8B%E6%8A%84&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Hokinaishō editions] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū - Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of Hokinaiden] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 Hokinaiden Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Origin Tales]

However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎|1=Yutu|2=ja|3=玉兎|lt=Gyokuto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutu|qq=}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition.
