According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|qq=}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pangu|qq=}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mañjuśrī|qq=}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

<!--Shinnaiden--> {{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ({{ill|Abe no Seimei|ja|安倍晴明|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Seimei|qq=}}). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the {{ill|Gyokuto|ja|玉兎|1=Yutu|2=ja|3=玉兎|lt=Gyokuto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyokuto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutu|qq=}}, a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by {{ill|Kibi no Makibi|ja|吉備真備|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Makibi|qq=}}."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|qq=}} and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity {{ill|Pangu|ja|盤古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pangu|qq=}} and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from {{ill|Mañjuśrī|ja|文殊菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mañjuśrī|qq=}} in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Gion Faith]] * [[Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū editions] * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=%E7%B0%A0%E7%B0%8B%E6%8A%84&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Hokinaishō editions] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū - Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of Hokinaiden] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 Hokinaiden Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Origin Tales]

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ([[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]]). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the [[:en:Yutu|Gyokuto]], a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by [[:en:Kibi no Makibi|Kibi no Makibi]]."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Gion cult|Gion Faith]] * [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ([[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]]). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the [[:en:Yutu|Gyokuto]], a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by [[:en:Kibi no Makibi|Kibi no Makibi]]."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Gion cult|Gion Faith]] * [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

[[sn:Shinnaiden]] [[ja:三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集]] [[en:Shinnaiden]]
<!--Shinnaiden--> {{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Gion Faith]] * [[Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū editions] * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=%E7%B0%A0%E7%B0%8B%E6%8A%84&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Hokinaishō editions] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū - Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of Hokinaiden] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 Hokinaiden Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Origin Tales]

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ([[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]]). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the [[:en:Yutu|Gyokuto]], a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by [[:en:Kibi no Makibi|Kibi no Makibi]]."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Gion cult|Gion Faith]] * [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

<!--Shinnaiden--> {{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Gion Faith]] * [[Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū editions] * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=%E7%B0%A0%E7%B0%8B%E6%8A%84&viewRestricted=0 National Diet Library - List of Hokinaishō editions] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū - Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of Hokinaiden] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 Hokinaiden Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Origin Tales]

It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.

<!--Shinnaiden--> {{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ([[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]]). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the [[:en:Yutu|Gyokuto]], a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by [[:en:Kibi no Makibi|Kibi no Makibi]]."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321

revision.text:
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ([[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]]). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the [[:en:Yutu|Gyokuto]], a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by [[:en:Kibi no Makibi|Kibi no Makibi]]."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Gion cult|Gion Faith]] * [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://dl.ndl.go.jp/search/searchResult?featureCode=all&searchWord=三國相傳陰陽 National Diet Library listings of *Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū*] * [http://codh.rois.ac.jp/pmjt/book/200019239/ *Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū* - Joint Usage Center for Humanities Open Data] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1520572357557459072 Religious World of *Hokinaiden*] * [https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1572543026800934912 *Hokinaiden* Notes: Relation to Gozu Tennō Legends]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321

[[Category:Shinto texts]]

'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a practical and specialized divination text traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei ([[:en:Abe no Seimei|Abe no Seimei]]). However, it is believed to have been compiled after Seimei's death, with various theories regarding its date of origin.
The work is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), abbreviated as '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), '''Hoki''' (簠簋), or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠 (Hoki)" and "簋 (Gui)" refer to ancient Chinese ritual vessels.
== Overview == The {{ill|Kin'u|ja|金烏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin'u|qq=}}, a three-legged golden crow associated with the sun, is a mythical bird symbolizing the sun, while the [[:en:Yutu|Gyokuto]], a rabbit said to dwell on the moon, represents the moon. These symbols reflect the content of this secret manual of onmyōji, which focuses on understanding the circulation of energy and divination based on the sun and moon's movements.
== Authorship == Although the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei, it was likely compiled after his death, casting doubt on this claim.
In the mid-Edo period text *Taizan-shū*, Abe no Yasufuku ({{ill|Abe no Yasufuku|ja|土御門泰福|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasufuku|qq=}}), the head of the Abe clan's onmyōdō school, is quoted as saying, "Hokinaiden was created by a Shingon monk and is not part of the Abe family tradition. Seimei passed down the astronomy brought back from Tang China by [[:en:Kibi no Makibi|Kibi no Makibi]]."
According to *Nihon Onmyōdō-shi Sōsetsu* (A General Overview of Japanese Onmyōdō), {{ill|Murayama Shūichi|ja|村山修一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shūichi|qq=}} speculates, based on explanations by {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, that the author may have been a shrine priest at Gion Shrine and a descendant of Seimei. After Abe no Yoshihira, Seimei's descendants split into three lines: Toki, Enya, and {{ill|Abe no Yasuchika|ja|安倍泰親|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Yasuchika|qq=}}. It is suggested that a descendant of Enya who joined Gion Shrine, specifically Seichō, might be the author of *Hokinaiden*.
However, {{ill|Nakamura Shōhachi|ja|中村璋八|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura Shōhachi|qq=}}, who conducted a comprehensive study of the text, states that there is no definitive evidence supporting Seichō as the author, leaving the authorship unresolved.
== Structure == The text consists of five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Explains the origins of [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and various {{ill|directional deities|ja|方位神|1=Directional deity|1=Directional deity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/directional deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Directional deity|qq=}} and their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Details the origins of the primordial deity [[:en:Pangu|Pangu]] and his progeny, as well as calendar-based divinations. * '''Volume 3''': Discusses additional elements such as {{ill|Nayin|ja|納音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nayin|qq=}} and {{ill|Kūbō|ja|空亡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūbō|qq=}}, not covered in the previous volumes. * '''Volume 4''': Focuses on [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Covers {{ill|Sukuyōdō|lt=Sukuyōdō|lt=Esoteric Buddhist astrology|ja|密教占星術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Esoteric Buddhist astrology|qq=}}, specifically {{ill|Shuku-yo|ja|宿曜占術|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuku-yo|qq=}}.
{{独自研究範囲|Volumes 4 and 5 are markedly different in style from the earlier volumes, suggesting they were later additions.|date=2017th year 8月}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" == The text includes a legend titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nittō-den" (天文司郎安部博士吉備后胤清明朝臣入唐伝).<ref>{{NDLDC|1911340/4}}</ref> It recounts the origins of the text, attributing it to knowledge passed from [[:en:Manjushri|Mañjuśrī]] in India to Haku-dō Shōnin, then to Abe no Seimei.<ref>*Hokinaiden* manuscripts vary, with some omitting the full narrative.</ref>
== Hokishō == *Hokishō* (簠簋抄) is a commentary on *Hokinaiden* published by the early Edo period. Another earlier commentary, *Hokisōriden* (簠簋袖裡伝), also exists.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Ippo, *Abe Seimei "Hokinaiden" Modern Translation and Commentary*, Eibunsha, 2017, ISBN 978-4864032636. * Shimode Tsuneyo (ed.), *Shintō Taikei 103: Onmyōdō*, Shintō Taikei Hensankai.

<!--Shinnaiden--> {{Refimprove|date=July 2019}}
'''Sangoku Sōden Onmyō Kankatsu Hokinaiden Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (三国相伝陰陽輨轄簠簋内伝金烏玉兎集) is a divination manual attributed to [[Abe no Seimei]]. While it is said to have been compiled by Seimei, the text was actually created after his death, with varying theories regarding its exact date of origin.
It is also referred to as '''Sangoku Sōden Senmyōrekikyōchū''' (三国相伝宣明暦経註), '''Hokinaiden''' (簠簋内伝), or simply '''Hoki''' (簠簋) or '''Kin'u Gyokuto-shū''' (金烏玉兎集).
The term "簠簋" refers to ceremonial vessels used in ancient China for offerings.
== Overview == The [[Golden Crow]] (金烏) symbolizes the sun, depicted as a three-legged golden crow residing in or embodying the sun. The [[Jade Rabbit]] (玉兎), associated with the moon, is a mythical rabbit said to dwell within it. These symbols represent the circulation of qi and divination based on the movements of the sun and moon, emphasizing the esoteric nature of this Onmyōdō text.
== Authorship == The authorship of the text is traditionally attributed to Abe no Seimei. However, as the text was compiled posthumously, its authenticity is questionable.
According to the mid-Edo period text "Taizan-shū," [[Tsunemasa Abe]], then head of the Abe family, dismissed the text as fabricated by a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] monk and not part of the Abe family’s tradition. Tsunemasa asserted that Seimei’s teachings were based on astronomy brought back by [[Kibi no Makibi]] from [[Tang China]].
[[Murayama Shūichi]] (as cited in "History of Japanese Onmyōdō") speculates, based on [[Nishida Nagao]]’s explanation, that the text’s author might have been a descendant of Seimei who served as a priest at [[Gion Shrine]]. Specifically, it is suggested that the text’s author was Harutomo, a descendant of Seimei’s lineage through [[Abe no Yoshihira]], who integrated into Gion Shrine.
[[Nakamura Shōhachi]] analyzed existing manuscripts and published versions, concluding that the attribution to Harutomo lacks definitive evidence, leaving the authorship uncertain.
== Structure == The text is divided into five volumes: * '''Volume 1''': Discusses the origins of [[Gozu Tennō]] and various [[directional deities]] with their auspicious and inauspicious influences. * '''Volume 2''': Covers the myth of [[Pangu]] and his offspring, explaining calendars and their omens. * '''Volume 3''': Details previously omitted concepts such as [[Naon]] and [[Kūbō]]. * '''Volume 4''': Discusses [[feng shui]] and architectural auspices. * '''Volume 5''': Explores [[Mikkyō]] astrology, particularly [[Sukuyō]] divination.
{{Original research|Volumes 4 and 5 are notably different in style from the first three. It is thought that Volumes 1 and 2 were written first, with Volume 3 added later as a supplement. Volumes 4 and 5 are believed to have been added separately.|date=August 2017}}
== "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden" == The text includes an anecdote titled "Tenmon Shirō Abe Hakase Kibi Kōin Seimei Chōjin Nyūtōden,"<ref>Featured at the beginning of Volume 3 in the Tanaka Taiemon edition held by the National Diet Library. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1911340/4 Link].</ref> which explains its origins. The story recounts that the text was transmitted by [[Monju Bosatsu]] in [[India]] to Hakudō Shōnin, who then passed it to Abe no Seimei.
The narrative states that during the [[Angen era]] (1175–1177), Seimei traveled to Tang China to study under Hakudō Shōnin and received the text, which he brought back to Japan and stored in a stone box.
The text also includes dramatic tales of betrayal, murder, and resurrection involving Seimei, his wife Rika, and his disciple [[Dōman]], emphasizing the sacred and secretive nature of the manual.
== Hokinaishō == The '''Hokinaishō''' is a commentary on the Kin'u Gyokuto-shū published by the early Edo period. It expands upon the original’s preface and incorporates folklore and anecdotes to enhance its narrative.
== Published Editions == * Fujimaki Kazuho, "Abe no Seimei: Hokinaiden Modern Translation and Commentary," Eibunkōsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4864032636 * Shitade Tsumiya, annotated, "Shintō Taikei 103 Onmyōdō" * Japanese Classical Pseudepigrapha Series, Volume 3, Modern Chōshinkyōsha. ISBN 9784329004321
